title: Menopause, hot flushes and sleep
excerpt: Menopause brings real physical changes, and Ayurveda has a name and a framework for them. Here is what the classical texts say, and what you can do every day to feel steadier.
at_a_glance: Menopause is a natural Vata-Pitta transition, not a deficiency, and Ayurveda offers a framework for it.
A stable daily rhythm, warm nourishing food, and regular self-oil-massage are the classical first steps.
Rebuilding Ojas through rest and gentle Rasayana therapies is at the heart of the classical approach.

body.heading: what your body is going through
paragraphs: Around your mid-forties or early fifties, you may notice that sleep starts breaking at odd hours, a wave of heat rises from your chest to your face for no apparent reason, and your mood shifts more quickly than it used to. These are not signs that something has gone wrong. They are signs that your body is crossing a significant threshold.

Ayurveda calls this transition Rajonivrutti, meaning the cessation of menstruation. The classical texts frame it not as a deficiency but as a natural movement through the three great stages of life. Childhood is governed primarily by Kapha dosha; the active middle decades by Pitta; and the later years increasingly by Vata (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Menopause is the hinge between the Pitta years and the Vata years, and that overlap is exactly what produces the symptoms you feel.
body.pullQuote: Menopause is the hinge between the Pitta years and the Vata years, and that overlap is exactly what produces the symptoms you feel.

body.heading: the ayurvedic view: vata, pitta and the shift
paragraphs: Hot flushes, night sweats, and irritability are understood as signs of Pitta aggravation, particularly of the sub-type called Bhrajaka Pitta, which governs heat in the skin and the flush that rises to the face (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). At the same time, Vata begins to increase, bringing its own signature: broken sleep, dryness, anxiety, and a feeling of groundlessness. When both doshas are simultaneously provoked, the body feels caught between fire and wind.

The texts also speak of Ojas, the refined essence of all bodily tissues, as the substance that keeps immunity, stamina, and emotional steadiness intact (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). The hormonal shift of menopause corresponds, in Ayurvedic terms, to a period when Ojas can become depleted if the woman is also carrying high stress, poor sleep, or an irregular diet. Rebuilding Ojas is therefore central to the classical approach to this transition.

Ashtanga Hridaya gives considerable attention to Rasayana, the category of nourishing therapies aimed at rejuvenating tissues and restoring vitality (Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana). These are not heroic interventions but gentle, consistent nourishment, the opposite of the aggressive cleansing protocols sometimes associated with Ayurveda.

body.heading: everyday habits that actually help
paragraphs: The most consistent recommendation across both Caraka Samhita and Ashtanga Hridaya for aggravated Vata and Pitta is the principle of Dinacharya, a stable daily rhythm (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana). Going to bed and rising at the same time each day does more for broken sleep than almost any single herb or remedy.

Warm, slightly oily food at regular mealtimes calms Vata. For Pitta, the key is avoiding the accumulation of heat: spicy food, heated arguments, and very hot environments all add to the fire. A glass of warm milk with a small pinch of saffron or a few strands of ashwagandha powder at night is a simple Rasayana that many women find settles both heat and anxiety before sleep.

Abhyanga, a self-massage with warm sesame oil before a bath, is recommended in Ashtanga Hridaya specifically for grounding Vata and lubricating the tissues (Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana). It takes ten to fifteen minutes and can meaningfully reduce the dryness and nervous restlessness that accompany this transition. For the heat side, a small amount of coconut oil can be applied to the soles of the feet and the crown of the head in the evening.

Gentle, consistent movement matters more than intense exercise at this stage. Walking in the morning, slow yoga, or swimming are all Vata-Pitta-friendly. Avoid exercising during the hottest part of the day and stop before you reach exhaustion, which the texts associate with a direct depletion of Ojas (Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana).

body.heading: when to see a doctor
paragraphs: The information here is general and is not a diagnosis or a substitute for medical care. Please see a qualified doctor or vaidya if your hot flushes are very frequent or severe and are preventing you from sleeping or working; if you experience chest pain, palpitations, or shortness of breath along with the flushes; if your periods become very heavy or irregular in a way that concerns you before they stop; if you notice significant mood changes, persistent low mood, or anxiety that affects daily life; or if you have a personal or family history of osteoporosis, cardiovascular disease, or breast cancer. A qualified practitioner can assess whether Ayurvedic support is appropriate alongside any other care you are receiving.

try_this_today.title: Warm milk before bed
try_this_today.description: Heat a cup of whole milk until just steaming. Stir in a small pinch of saffron or half a teaspoon of ashwagandha powder. Drink slowly about thirty minutes before sleep. This is one of the simplest Rasayana practices from the classical tradition.

try_this_today.title: Ten-minute morning oil massage
try_this_today.description: Before your bath, warm a small amount of sesame oil and massage it into your limbs with long strokes, using circular motions at the joints. This grounds Vata and lubricates dry tissues. Rinse off in a warm (not hot) shower.

try_this_today.title: Cool your feet in the evening
try_this_today.description: Apply a small amount of coconut oil to the soles of your feet and the crown of your head before bed. This helps dissipate accumulated Pitta heat and often improves sleep quality within a week of consistent practice.

try_this_today.title: Keep mealtimes fixed
try_this_today.description: Eat your largest meal at midday when digestion is strongest and a lighter, warm meal in the early evening. Skipping meals or eating very late aggravates both Vata and Pitta. Consistency of timing is, according to the Caraka Samhita, one of the pillars of Dinacharya.

try_this_today.title: Morning walk before the heat rises
try_this_today.description: A thirty-minute walk in the cooler morning air is ideal for this stage of life. It moves Vata without provoking Pitta and, according to the classical texts, preserves Bala (strength and vitality) without depleting Ojas. Stop well before exhaustion.

words_to_know.definition: Cessation of menstruation; the Ayurvedic term for menopause, framed as a natural life transition rather than a disease.
The refined essence of all seven bodily tissues; governs immunity, stamina, and emotional resilience.
A category of nourishing therapies aimed at rejuvenating tissues and restoring vitality; preventive and restorative rather than curative.
Daily routine; the structured rhythm of sleep, meals, movement, and self-care that the classical texts place at the foundation of health.
Therapeutic self-massage with warm oil; recommended in Ashtanga Hridaya for grounding Vata and nourishing the tissues.
The sub-type of Pitta that governs heat and lustre in the skin; its aggravation produces the flushing and heat waves of menopause.

sources: Caraka Samhita, Sutrasthana · Ashtanga Hridaya, Sutrasthana · Ashtanga Hridaya, Uttarasthana
disclaimer: Ayurveda guidance on AyurHQ is supportive care, not emergency or diagnostic medical treatment.
